perturbation.original_text: Nexstar shall maintain the Equipment in good order and repair in accordance with industry standards.
perturbation.changed_text: Nexstar shall endeavor to maintain the Equipment in generally acceptable condition.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires Nexstar to maintain equipment according to 'industry standards,' a relatively clear benchmark. The modified text replaces this with 'endeavor to maintain' and 'generally acceptable condition,' both of which are vague and subjective. This weakens the obligation and opens it to interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to maintain equipment
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text uses 'endeavor' and 'generally acceptable condition,' making the obligation vague. Under Illinois law (810 ILCS 5/2-302), contracts must have clear and definite terms. The ambiguity of 'endeavor' and 'generally acceptable' could make it difficult to prove a breach of contract because these terms are subjective and lack a clear standard for enforcement, potentially leading to unfair or unconscionable outcomes.

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'in accordance with industry standards' to 'endeavor to maintain in generally acceptable condition' introduces ambiguity, which weakens the original obligation. The LLM explanation points to Illinois law requiring contracts to have clear and definite terms (810 ILCS 5/2-302). Although the scraped law snippet does not contain the exact text mentioned by the LLM, the provided snippet relating to Illinois Compiled Statutes indicates the existence of laws governing business transactions and contracts within Illinois. The modification introduces ambiguity and subjectivity making it difficult to enforce and thus creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall make a payment to the other with respect to certain mutually agreed upon expenses and capital expenditures incurred (or to be incurred) as a result of the relationship created by this Agreement, such payments to be made promptly following the incurrence of any expenses and/or expenditures.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may consider making a payment to the other with respect to expenses and capital expenditures if they feel it is appropriate as a result of the relationship created by this Agreement, such payments to be made in a timely manner if possible.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates an obligation for each party to make payments. The revised text changes 'shall make a payment' to 'may consider making a payment if they feel it is appropriate,' making the payment discretionary. 'Promptly' is replaced with 'in a timely manner if possible,' further weakening the payment obligation and making it subjective.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to make payments
perturbation.law_explanation: Illinois law requires good faith and commercial reasonableness in contract performance (810 ILCS 5/2-305). Changing 'shall make a payment' to 'may consider making a payment if they feel it is appropriate' introduces an element of subjective discretion that could be used to avoid payment, contradicting the implied duty of good faith and creating an unenforceable term.

For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 810 ILCS 5/2-305 (810 ILCS 5/2-305) (from Ch. 26, par. 2-305) Sec. 2-305. Open price term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. (Source: Laws 1961, p. 2101 .) perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates payment ('shall make a payment'), while the modified language makes payment discretionary ('may consider making a payment if they feel it is appropriate'). This change contradicts the implied duty of good faith, potentially allowing a party to avoid payment, which is against commercial reasonableness. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 810 ILCS 5/2-305 (2), states that 'A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith.' By making the payment discretionary, the modified text opens the door to a party not acting in good faith, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Nexstar shall use commercially reasonable efforts to collect the Accounts Receivable in the usual and ordinary course of business, using Nexstar's credit, sales and other appropriate personnel in accordance with customary practices, which are not required to include referral to a collection agency.
perturbation.changed_text: Nexstar might attempt to collect the Accounts Receivable as it deems fit, using its personnel as available.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear standard of 'commercially reasonable efforts' and specifies the use of appropriate personnel. The modified text uses 'might attempt' and 'as it deems fit' and 'as available,' making the collection effort entirely discretionary and removing any specific standard of care. This effectively eliminates any real obligation to collect the receivables.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to collect receivables with due diligence
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation for Nexstar to collect accounts receivable. Under Illinois law (810 ILCS 5/1-304), every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. By changing 'shall use commercially reasonable efforts' to 'might attempt to collect the Accounts Receivable as it deems fit,' the contract allows Nexstar to potentially shirk its duty to collect, conflicting with the implied covenant of good faith and violating the law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'commercially reasonable efforts' for collecting Accounts Receivable, whereas the changed contract uses permissive language ('might attempt') and subjective criteria ('as it deems fit,' 'as available'). The Law Explanation says that under Illinois law (810 ILCS 5/1-304), every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance. The scraped snippet 1 identifies an Illinois law: '815 ILCS 605/ Credit Services Organizations Act.' While this specific citation isn't directly relevant to the good faith argument from the Law Explanation, the shift to permissive language weakens the original obligation. This makes the collection effort discretionary, which contradicts the implied covenant of good faith that the LLM refers to. Because the modification removes the standard of care, it is a contradiction.
